title: Shared Leads Convert at 6-10%. Direct Calls at 40%. Do the Math | Booked Job
h1: Shared leads convert at 6-10%. A direct call converts at up to 40%. Do the math
meta: Shared leads close at 6-10%, organic at 18-24%, direct calls up to 40% (2026 lead-network data). Why buying Angi/Thumbtack leads is a rigged race — and what to buy instead.
short: Shared leads from Angi and Thumbtack close at just 6-10% because you're racing 4-12 other pros for the same call. Organic leads convert at 18-24%, and a direct call converts at up to 40% (2026 lead-network data). Owned channels convert far better than the leads you buy shared. Here's the math.
statLabel: Conversion rate on a direct call vs 6-10% on a shared lead (2026 lead-network data)

body.answer: Here's the whole game in one line.
body.html: Here's the whole game in one line. Shared leads convert at just 6-10%, organic leads at 18-24%, and a direct call at up to 40% (2026 lead-network data). That means the channels you own convert several times better than the shared leads you buy from Angi or Thumbtack. The reason shared leads lose is simple: you're racing 4-12 other pros for the exact same call.

body.h2: Why shared leads are a rigged game
body.answer: When you buy a shared lead, you're not buying a customer.
body.html:
When you buy a shared lead, you're not buying a customer. You're buying a ticket to a footrace. The 2026 lead-network data is blunt about why shared leads convert worst: you're racing 4-12 other pros for the same homeowner. Thumbtack sends the same lead to 4-5 pros; Angi/HomeAdvisor resells to a pack of competitors too. That's why shared leads top out at a 6-10% close rate — a crowd of you are calling the same person in the same hour.

Now stack the first-responder reality on top. Lead Connect (2026) found that 78% of customers hire the first contractor to respond. So on a shared lead, the platform has already handed your competitors a head start on the same phone number. You can be the best plumber in town and still lose because someone dialed moments sooner.

body.h2: The cost-per-booked-job math nobody shows you
body.answer: Cost per lead is a vanity number.
body.html:
Cost per lead is a vanity number. Cost per booked job is the only one that pays your crew. Run the real figures: Google LSA lands around $168 per booked job because those leads are exclusive (SearchLight Digital, 2026). Thumbtack runs about $250 per booked job, shared with 4-5 pros. Angi/HomeAdvisor runs about $542 per booked job — the same company as HomeAdvisor — with customer acquisition cost climbing as high as ~$2,500 (2026 lead-network comparisons).

Angi piles on more drag: a $300-500/yr membership on top of per-lead costs, and 15-22% of leads refunded as credits, not cash, so you pay first and chase the credit later (2026). Owned SEO, by contrast, runs about $290-310 per booked job today and gets cheaper every year as the asset compounds (2026). The low sticker price on a shared lead hides the worst cost-per-booked-job on the board.

body.answer: Speed matters everywhere, but it only pays off when the lead is yours.
body.html:
Speed matters everywhere, but it only pays off when the lead is yours. MIT Sloan (2026) found that responding within 5 minutes makes a lead 100x more likely to qualify than waiting 30 minutes. And 78% of customers hire the first contractor to respond (Lead Connect, 2026).

On a shared lead, that speed advantage gets neutralized — the platform blasted the same lead to a pack of pros simultaneously, so your fast reply is racing other fast replies. On a direct call or an organic inquiry, you're the only number the customer has. That's why the same fast follow-up that's worth almost nothing on a shared lead is worth a booked job on an owned one. Don't torch it by missing the phone, either: CallRail (2026) puts the home-services missed-call rate at 14% — about one in seven calls goes unanswered.

body.h2: Where to move the money instead
body.html:
The move isn't to spend less. 72% of contractors are increasing marketing budgets in 2026, and 62% say lead generation is their single biggest challenge (CallRail, 2026). The move is to point the same dollars at channels that convert far better and that you actually own.

Start with the ones that stack: exclusive paid leads (LSA at ~$168 per booked job), your own website, and your Google reviews. The stakes on reviews are real — 91% of people read local reviews and most won't consider a business under 4 stars (BrightLocal, 2025), and 81% rely on Google reviews to decide (CallRail, 2026). One warning on your website: typical contractor sites convert at just 2-3%, so ~98% of visitors leave without contacting you (WebFX, 2026). Owned doesn't mean automatic — it means the upside is yours to capture instead of split with a crowd.

Here's what most contractors miss. The 6-10% close rate on shared leads isn't a reflection of your sales skill or your follow-up speed. It's a structural ceiling baked into the product. When a lead is sold to 4-12 pros, math caps your share of the wins — and no amount of hustle raises that ceiling, because the other pros are hustling on the exact same phone number (2026 lead-network data).

Flip it: the reason a direct call converts at up to 40% and organic at 18-24% isn't that those customers are somehow better. It's that you're the only pro in the conversation, so your speed and your reviews actually pay off. The lesson isn't 'never buy leads.' It's 'never buy a lead you have to share.' Buy exclusive, build owned, and let your speed do what it can't do in a crowded race.

faq.q: Why do shared leads convert so much worse than direct calls?
faq.a: Because a shared lead is sold to a crowd. The 2026 lead-network data shows shared leads close at 6-10% versus up to 40% on a direct call, specifically because you're racing 4-12 other pros for the same homeowner. Thumbtack shares each lead with 4-5 pros, and Angi resells to a pack of competitors too (2026). On a direct call you're the only number the customer has, so your speed and reviews actually decide the job.

faq.q: Are shared leads from Angi or Thumbtack ever worth buying?
faq.a: They're the worst deal on the cost-per-booked-job math. Thumbtack runs ~$250 per booked job and Angi/HomeAdvisor ~$542 (same company as HomeAdvisor), with CAC as high as ~$2,500 (2026). Angi also charges a $300-500/yr membership and refunds 15-22% of leads as credits, not cash (2026). Exclusive LSA leads land around $168 per booked job by comparison (SearchLight Digital, 2026).

faq.q: If I stop buying shared leads, where should the budget go?
faq.a: Toward exclusive and owned channels that convert far better: Google LSA (~$168 per booked job, exclusive), owned SEO (~$290-310 per booked job and falling as it compounds), your own website, and your Google reviews. 91% of people read reviews before hiring (BrightLocal, 2025) and 81% rely on Google reviews to decide (CallRail, 2026). Just fix your site first — typical contractor sites convert at only 2-3% (WebFX, 2026).

faq.q: Does responding fast fix the shared-lead problem?
faq.a: Not really. A 5-minute response is 100x more likely to qualify a lead than a 30-minute one (MIT Sloan, 2026), and 78% hire whoever responds first (Lead Connect, 2026) — but on a shared lead the platform sent the same lead to a pack of pros at once, so your fast reply is racing their fast replies. Speed only pays off when the lead is exclusively yours.

unsourcedClaims: Angi/HomeAdvisor to 2-4 pros (2026) — no stat in the database gives a per-lead resale count for Angi (only Thumbtack's 4-5 is sourced); removed.
'4-6x better' multiplier — not a stat in the database; it is a synthesized ratio. Replaced with qualitative 'several times better' throughout.
'90 seconds sooner' — invented precision not sourced anywhere; replaced with qualitative 'moments sooner'.
'four pros'/'four-way race'/'split four ways' — the '4' is illustrative shorthand for the sourced 4-12 range; softened to avoid stating an unsupported exact count.
_verdict.notes: Cross-checked every figure against content/stats.json (the actual sourced stats DB; the prompt's 'undefined' allowed-list was empty so I used the repo's canonical database). Sourced and kept: shared 6-10%/organic 18-24%/direct up to 40% (shared-convert), LSA ~$168 (lsa-cpbj), Thumbtack ~$250 and 4-5 pros (thumbtack-cpbj), Angi ~$542 + CAC ~$2,500 (angi-cpbj), SEO ~$290-310 (seo-cpbj), 78% first-responder (first-responder), 100x/5-min-vs-30 (5min-100x), 14%/~1-in-7 missed calls (missed-call), 2-3%/~98% site (site-convert), 91%/<4-stars (reviews-read), 81% Google reviews (google-reviews-decide), 72%/62% (budgets-up), $300-500/yr membership (angi-fee), 15-22% refunds (angi-refund), 'racing 4-12 other pros' (shared-convert note). REFUTED/FIXED: (1) 'Angi/HomeAdvisor to 2-4 pros' — no source for an Angi resale count; removed. (2) '4-6x better' — derived, not a DB stat; converted to qualitative 'several times better'. (3) '90 seconds sooner' — invented precision; made qualitative. (4) 'four pros'/'split four ways' phrasing softened where it implied an exact count. Tone is on-brand (blunt). allNumbersSourced=false because unsourced claims were present and required correction.
